one_line_takeaway: A UPerNet-based network combining dual Vision-Mamba (VSSD) backbones with a novel ground-truth-guided Graph Neural Network module unifies shallowfake and deepfake image-manipulation localization/detection in one model, beating CAT-Net, ObjectFormer, PSCC-Net, and Mesorch baselines on combined pixel-level F1 (0.8104) and image-level F1/AUC (0.8653/0.9320) when trained jointly on both manipulation types.
key_finding: On the combined shallow+deepfake test set, trained jointly on both domains, the proposed method achieved the best overall results: pixel-level F1 0.8104, image-level F1 0.8653, image-level AUC 0.9320 -- roughly 5 percentage points higher pixel-level F1 than the second-best baseline, CAT-Net (0.7642 combined not directly stated as such but derivable from Table II row 15 vs CAT-Net row 3: 0.7642). On the shallowfake-only subset the method ranked first in pixel-level F1 (0.6830) but slightly behind ObjectFormer in image-level F1 on that subset (0.7402 vs 0.8028 for ObjectFormer trained on shallowfakes only). On the deepfake-only subset it ranked second in pixel-level F1 (0.9444, behind own single-domain-trained variant 0.9436 vs. e.g. PSCC-Net 0.8115/ObjectFormer 0.7851 trained on deepfakes alone) while achieving the best image-level F1 (0.9699) and AUC (0.9945) among joint-trained models. Ablations (Table IV, all models trained on both domains) show VSSD backbone alone improves over their prior ResNet50 baseline by about 4% combined pixel/image F1 (0.7511->0.8000 pixel F1 change reported as ~4%), and adding G-GNN (vs. unguided GNN or VSSD-only) adds roughly another 1% combined, with larger ~2-4% gains specifically on the shallowfake subset. Under Gaussian noise distortion the method has the best average pixel-level F1 (0.7468 vs. CAT-Net 0.7428); under Gaussian blur and JPEG compression it ranks second-best average (0.6175 and 0.6970 respectively, behind CAT-Net's 0.6053/0.7475 for blur/JPEG... note CAT-Net is actually higher on blur average 0.6053 is lower than theirs' 0.6175 -- so proposed method is best on blur average too by the reported numbers, and second on JPEG where Mesorch's 0.6888 is close but the paper states 'second best against Gaussian blur and JPEG compression' referring to specific baselines). Model size: 49.50M learnable parameters, smaller than CAT-Net (114.26M) and Mesorch (85.75M) but larger than ObjectFormer (34.41M) and PSCC-Net (3.67M).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a unified deep-learning network that jointly detects and localizes both 'shallowfake' (traditional editing: splicing, copy-move, inpainting) and 'deepfake' (AI-generated face manipulation) tampering in still images, without needing to know the manipulation type in advance. The architecture is built on the UPerNet segmentation framework and has two specific novel elements the authors claim as contributions: (1) using two Vision Mamba (VSSD, the 'Visual State Space Duality' backbone from Shi et al. 2024) branches -- one on the raw image, one on a BayarConv-extracted noise map -- to get a larger effective receptive field than their own prior ResNet50-based system (Zhang et al., ICNC 2023, cited as their own earlier work [29]); and (2) a novel Guided Graph Neural Network (G-GNN) module that extends Vision GNN (ViG) by using ground-truth manipulation masks (available only at training time) to guide graph-node connectivity via a triplet loss, so that graph neighbors are pulled toward same-class (real/real or fake/fake) pixels and pushed away from opposite-class pixels.
threat_model: This is a forensic/detection paper, not an adversarial-attack paper: it does not define an adversary who attacks the model. Instead it addresses the problem of localizing manipulated pixel regions in images that may have been altered either by conventional editing tools (splicing, copy-move, inpainting) or by AI-based deepfake generation methods (Deepfakes, Face2Face, FaceSwap, NeuralTextures, FaceShifter), motivated by concerns about misinformation and reputational harm from manipulated media spread on social platforms. It does include a robustness evaluation against non-adversarial signal distortions (Gaussian noise, Gaussian blur, JPEG compression at varying severities) to test whether localization degrades under common image post-processing, but this is not framed or tested as an adaptive/white-box adversarial attack.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) no existing image dataset provides ground-truth manipulation masks for deepfakes, so the authors had to construct one from FaceForensics++ video frames and mask videos themselves, discarding color information from computer-graphics-based mask videos to binarize them, and omitting 1,000 FaceShifter videos entirely because no masks were available for them; some videos also could not be downloaded due to accessibility issues. (2) Guided masks used by G-GNN require ground-truth labels and are therefore usable only during training, not inference -- a design constraint acknowledged explicitly. (3) Comparison baselines (CAT-Net, ObjectFormer, PSCC-Net, Mesorch) were selected specifically because their training code was public, which the authors state is needed for fair re-training on the same data, but this selection criterion could exclude other strong methods. (4) Under Gaussian blur and JPEG compression distortion, their method is only second-best on average despite not being trained with any distortion augmentation, which the authors present as a caveat alongside the noise-robustness result. (5) No adversarial robustness (adaptive/white-box attack) evaluation is performed, only non-adversarial common-corruption robustness.

datasets_used: CASIAv2 (training, shallowfake: 7,490 authentic / 4,948 manipulated images, splicing+copy-move, split 8:1:1)

FaceForensics++ (deepfake source: 1,363 authentic YouTube videos, 5,000 fake videos via Deepfakes, Face2Face, FaceShifter, FaceSwap, NeuralTextures; authors extracted frames themselves -- 8,449 authentic and 7,330 forged frames total after excluding FaceShifter for lack of masks and some inaccessible videos)
quantitative_results: Proposed method (trained on both domains), combined test set: pixel-level F1 0.8104, image-level F1 0.8653, image-level AUC 0.9320 (Table II row 15) -- best overall among compared methods
Proposed method, shallowfake-only test subset (joint-trained): pixel-level F1 0.6830 (best), image-level F1 0.7402 (second to ObjectFormer's 0.8028 when ObjectFormer trained shallowfake-only)
Proposed method, deepfake-only test subset (joint-trained): pixel-level F1 0.9444 (second-best), image-level F1 0.9699 and AUC 0.9945 (both best among joint-trained models)

Ablation (Table IV, combined test set): ResNet50 backbone (prior work) pixel F1 0.7511/image F1 0.8170/AUC 0.9177 -> VSSD backbone 0.8000/0.8523/0.9260 -> VSSD+GNN(no guidance) 0.8034/0.8544/0.9126 -> VSSD+G-GNN (final) 0.8104/0.8653/0.9320

Robustness averages, pixel-level F1 on combined distorted test set (Table V): Gaussian noise avg -- Ours 0.7468 vs CAT-Net 0.7428 (best); Gaussian blur avg -- Ours 0.6175 vs CAT-Net 0.6053, Mesorch 0.6415 (Mesorch best on blur); JPEG avg -- Ours 0.6970 vs CAT-Net 0.7475 (CAT-Net best on JPEG)
Learnable parameters (Table VI): CAT-Net 114.26M, ObjectFormer 34.41M, PSCC-Net 3.67M, Mesorch 85.75M, Ours 49.50M
baselines_compared: PSCC-Net (progressive spatio-channel correction, Liu et al. 2022)
Mesorch (DCT + CNN/Transformer mixed, Zhu et al. 2025)
Authors' own prior ResNet50-backbone system (Zhang et al., ICNC 2023) used as ablation baseline, not a Table II external baseline
